message.content: Adversaries may exploit the Windows Control Panel process to execute malicious payloads by abusing Control Panel items, which are typically executable (.exe) or Control Panel (.cpl) files. These .cpl files are essentially renamed dynamic-link libraries (.dll) that can be executed directly from the command line, via API calls, or by double-clicking. This technique can be used to bypass application and file extension allow lists, making it a favored method in phishing campaigns and multi-stage malware attacks. Notable examples include Ember Bear, InvisiMole, and Reaver, which have used malicious CPL files for execution and persistence. Mitigation strategies include monitoring and restricting the execution of Control Panel items, implementing strict allow lists, and employing endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions to detect and block suspicious activity related to Control Panel files. Regularly updating and patching systems can also help prevent exploitation through this vector.
